description:
You are working as a Linux systems-engineer who has to “harden” a server by slightly lowering a few *ulimit*-style resource caps.  
    A small “optimization solver” must be written to calculate the new caps (the objective is to minimise every limit while
    respecting the security constraint that **no value may drop below 1024**).

    1. A CSV file already exists at  
       /home/user/security/limits.csv

       Current contents:

       Parameter,Current
       nofile,4096
       nproc,8192
       memlock,16384
       fsize,1048576

    2. Create a Python 3 program called  
       /home/user/security/optimize_limits.py  
       that performs the following optimisation for every row:

         new_value = max( floor(Current * 0.90) , 1024 )

       • Use integer arithmetic (truncate/​floor any non-integer result).  
       • Preserve the original ordering of the parameters.

    3. When the program is executed it must:  
       • Read /home/user/security/limits.csv  
       • Write /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv with exactly this **comma-separated** structure (no superfluous spaces):

         Parameter,Optimized
         nofile,3686
         nproc,7372
         memlock,14745
         fsize,943718

       • Append one single-line entry to /home/user/security/optimization_run.log having the exact format:

         YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - Optimization completed. 4 parameters processed. Output: /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv

         where the timestamp is the local system time when the script finishes.  Do NOT write anything else to the log file.

    4. Verify that both /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv and
       /home/user/security/optimization_run.log exist and contain the
       precise information described above.

    Hints:  
    • The task can be accomplished with roughly three or four shell commands
      (create script, run it, inspect the results).  
    • You do not need any external libraries apart from the Python standard library.

    The automated grader will check:  
    • That /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv exists.  
    • That its **exact** byte contents match the CSV block shown above (newline at the end of each line, including the last one).  
    • That /home/user/security/optimization_run.log exists and contains exactly one line whose trailing static text is:

      "Optimization completed. 4 parameters processed. Output: /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv"

      (The grader will ignore the timestamp portion as long as it is in the format `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`).

    Remember: do not display the path contents in your reply; instead, issue the necessary terminal commands to create the script, run it, and review the files.
truth:
Initial files provided to the container before the agent starts:

    1. Directory: /home/user/security  (permissions 755, owner user)
    2. File: /home/user/security/limits.csv
       Exact contents (byte-for-byte, final line ends with newline):
Parameter,Current
nofile,4096
nproc,8192
memlock,16384
fsize,1048576

    Successful completion requirements:

    A. New file: /home/user/security/optimized_limits.csv
       Exact contents (byte-for-byte, final line ends with newline):
Parameter,Optimized
nofile,3686
nproc,7372
memlock,14745
fsize,943718

    B. Updated/created file: /home/user/security/optimization_run.log
       Must contain exactly ONE line that matches the regex  
       ^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2} - Optimization completed\. 4 parameters processed\. Output: /home/user/security/optimized_limits\.csv\n$
       (i.e. a trailing newline is mandatory).

    C. Python script: /home/user/security/optimize_limits.py
       Can be any implementation producing the required artefacts, but the grader will execute  
       `python3 /home/user/security/optimize_limits.py`  
       and expect it to terminate with exit status 0 while preserving A and appending a new log entry as in B (if run more than once, multiple correctly-formatted log lines are allowed).

    No other files or directories are modified or required by the tests.
